text: Hi guys, this is Dr. Andrew Iraniha from Surgical Oasis Institute. This video demonstrates my technique for robotic cholecystectomy.

This patient is a thirty-five year old female who had episodic abdominal pain in the right upper quadrant with abdominal bloating and her ultrasound showed evidence of a stone in the gallbladder consistent with a symptomatic cholelithiasis.

The surgeon places trocars and robotic instruments, then isolates the cystic artery from the surrounding peritoneum. Tiny branches of the artery are separated, cauterized, or clipped. The cystic artery is shown completely isolated, with clips applied to prevent postoperative bleeding.

The robotic surgery provides enhanced visualization, allowing the surgeon to perform minimally invasive procedures with small incisions. After ligating and dividing the cystic artery, the surgeon continues to separate the gallbladder from the liver using cautery. A small branch is clipped and divided before detaching the gallbladder from the hepatic artery and common bile duct. The surgeon creates a window known as the triangle of safety to ensure the cystic duct is isolated, placing clips around it to prevent bile leakage. The gallbladder is then separated from the liver and placed into a plastic pouch for removal through a small incision.

This procedure just took twenty minutes and patient went home after one hour of recovery She did very well after the surgery and two weeks later completely recovered and back to normal heavy exercise and activity.
If you have any questions about gallbladder surgery, please contact my office at nine four nine six four six eight four four four for consultation. Thank you.
